Beachwood Community Center drug disposal campaign · February 2026
About This Project

What started in a lab became something I couldn't ignore.

My name is So Eun Kang, and I am a rising senior at Beachwood High School. When I began researching how fentanyl disrupts immune cell function at Case Western Reserve University, I expected to stay in the lab. But the science led me outside.

The more I learned about opioids at the cellular level, the more I felt the need to understand the crisis at the community level. There was a gap between what exists and what people know. Naloxone, a medication that reverses overdose, was sitting behind pharmacy counters, tucked inside AED kits, and overshadowed by common painkillers. Most people around me had no idea.

So I started mapping it. I visited over 80 pharmacies and public spaces across Cleveland and Boston, wrote about it, taught people how to use it, and brought policy proposals to our school board to increase awareness of this life-saving medication at my own school.

📰 The Beachcomber SAFE Project CADCA CWRU Levine Lab
Naloxone

What you need to know, and why it matters now.

In 2023, over 105,000 Americans died from drug-involved overdoses. Naloxone can reverse an opioid overdose in minutes. And it's available (at most pharmacies) without a prescription.

01
What Is Naloxone?

Naloxone reverses an opioid overdose by blocking opioid receptors in the brain and restoring normal breathing within minutes. It has no effect if opioids are not present. Since 2023, naloxone has been FDA-approved for over-the-counter sale without a prescription.

02
How to Access Naloxone

Pharmacies: CVS and Walgreens carry naloxone over-the-counter Typical price: $44.99 for 2 doses (however, this varies greatly by region). Usually no prescription needed.

Public spaces: Check AED kits: many now contain naloxone.

03
How to Use Naloxone

Signs of an overdose: Unresponsive, slow/stopped breathing, blue lips, pale cold skin.

1. Call 911. 2. Lay person on back, tilt head. 3. Insert nozzle in nostril, press plunger. 4. If no response in 2–3 min, administer second dose. 5. Recovery position until help arrives.

Narcan found at a Cleveland-area pharmacy · Summer 2025
Investigating naloxone placement at a Walgreens in Boston · Summer 2025
Policy Change

Two proposals, one school board.

The naloxone access map and community campaign data became the case for institutional change. I brought two proposals to the Beachwood school board. Both were approved.

Proposal 1: Installation

Naloxone boxes installed in school hallways, next to the AEDs kits.

1

Proposal drafted

Modeled after a setup observed at another local high school.

2

Presented to the board

Addressed cost, placement, and effectiveness questions.

✓

Passed

Naloxone boxes will now be installed next to AED units.

Proposal 2: Curriculum

Naloxone education formally added to the health curriculum.

1

Proposal drafted

Built on the pharmacy mapping and community campaign findings.

2

Presented to the board

Delivered with real data and potential benefits

✓

Passed

Naloxone and overdose response are now part of the health curriculum.

What this means: Beachwood High School will have both naloxone education built into the curriculum and physical naloxone access in the building. Implementation is underway.

The naloxone box installation and overdose response education integration, both approved by the school board

Red = Gap

Safe Drug Disposal

Unused medications at home are a risk to everyone.

42% of people who misuse prescription pain relievers get them from a friend or family member. Safe disposal starts at home.

How to Use a Deterra Pouch

At-home deactivation pouches let you safely dispose of unused medications any day.

1

Place medications inside

Pills, patches, liquids, creams, including opioids and controlled substances.

2

Add warm water halfway

Activated carbon inside binds to and neutralizes drug compounds.

3

Seal and shake 30 seconds

Carbon neutralizes harmful components, preventing environmental contamination.

4

Dispose in regular trash

Safe for normal garbage. Do not flush! It harms waterways.

!

Not for injectables

EpiPens and sharps require separate disposal programs.

Why This Matters

8.6M
Americans aged 12+ reported misusing prescription opioids in 2023 (CDC)
42%
Of those who misuse pain relievers get them from a friend or relative, often from a home medicine cabinet
19%→92%
Proper disposal rates when patients were given an at-home product and shown how to use it

From our community campaign: 90% had never heard of at-home disposal products. After education, 85% planned to use their pouch immediately. The barrier isn't resistance. It's awareness.

Get a Free Deterra Pouch (Ohio) →
Community drug disposal campaign: 78 participants, 80 Deterra pouches distributed · February 2026
Investigative Journalism

Four articles. One crisis. Reported from the inside.

As the incoming Editor-in-Chief of The Beachcomber, I published a four-part investigative series on the opioid crisis. One article received national recognition from Best of SNO.

01
Opioid Crisis
May 23, 2025
Despite Declines, Overdose Deaths Continue at Crisis Levels

Teen substance use trends, the fentanyl crisis, and what local health professionals are seeing on the ground.
02
Naloxone Access
September 25, 2025
Narcan Can Save Lives: But Only If You Know Where to Find It

Visiting locations from Cleveland to Boston to map how accessible naloxone really is.

⭐ Best of SNO
03
Policy & Change
December 19, 2025
How a BHS Grad Helped Bring Naloxone to Boston Subway Stations

Swathi Srinivasan (BHS '17) secured $95,000 in state funding to install naloxone across the MBTA Red Line.
04
Prevention
March 11, 2026
The Importance of Safe Drug Disposal

An investigation into awareness gaps, stigma, and what it takes to get unused medications out of medicine cabinets.
05
FDA Policy
FDA Docket · FDA-2026-N-1001-0067
Public Comment Submission Regarding In-Home Disposal Products

Commented and approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration via Regulations.gov.

National Advocacy

2026 CADCA Mid-Year Training Institute.

From July 12–16, 2026, I was in Orlando attending the CADCA Mid-Year Training Institute, where I met countless officials and people in advocacy paving the way for a Fentanyl Free America.

I was truly inspired to see so many people, including youth leaders from across the country, stand united in one place to combat a crisis that has taken away the lives of so many.

As an individual presenter at this event, I was able to inform other youth of how to advocate in their own communities and distributed a toolkit that they could utilize in order to expand their work.

However, I could not have expanded my outreach without the support of so many people around me fighting for the same cause, and it was inspiring to meet with officials from the Office of National Drug Control Policy, the Drug Enforcement Administration, CADCA, and so many more organizations discussing ways we can all make change.

Some of the wonderful people I met included: General Barrye Price, Cheri Oz, Terry Cole, Joseph O. Dixon, Brian McNeal, Helen Hernandez, Christopher Jakim, Sierra Talavera-Brown, and Ingrid Donato.

Educating student leaders from across the country on expanding advocacy work as an individual presenter for my 3.5-hour session
Meeting with CADCA CEO General Barrye Price

Get Help

If you or someone you know needs support.

Help is available 24/7. You don't need to hit rock bottom to reach out. The sooner, the better.

SAMHSA National Helpline

Free, confidential, 24/7 treatment referral and information for individuals and families facing substance use disorders.

1-800-662-4357
Federal
SAMHSA
FindTreatment.gov and FindSupport.gov for local resources nationwide.
Cuyahoga County
ADAMHS Board
Local treatment and recovery resources for Cuyahoga County residents.
Pepper Pike, OH
New Directions
Nonjudgmental outpatient and residential treatment, including MAT for opioid addiction.
Ohio
OhioRx: Free Deterra Pouches
Ohio residents can request free at-home drug disposal pouches.
National Nonprofit
SAFE Project
Ending the addiction fatality epidemic through prevention, recovery, and community action.
Crisis Line
988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
Call or text 988. Free, confidential support 24/7.
